project_overview.project_name: South Sudan Health Sector Transformation Project (HSTP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To expand access to a basic package of health and nutrition services, improve health sector stewardship, and strengthen the health system
project_overview.total_financing: US$369.50 million (SDR 89.1 million equivalent, of which SDR 80 million from Window for Host Communities and Refugees)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Population of South Sudan (12.4 million), particularly women of reproductive age and children under five; approximately 330,000 refugees and host communities; 1,158 health facilities (67% of total facilities)

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - South Sudan is classified as fragile, conflict-affected and violence-prone (FCV). The country has experienced only about 15 years of peace since 1955 (1972-1982 and 2005-2011). Civil war erupted in 2013. Current context includes ongoing intercommunal conflicts, security challenges in Upper Nile, Jonglei, and Unity states, and recent refugee influx from Sudan conflict (April 2023 onwards)

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Provision of Basic Health Services Nationwide
key_components.description: Delivery of basic health and nutrition services through health facilities and community-based Boma Health Initiative (BHI), including maternal and neonatal health, child health, nutrition, immunization, family planning, SGBV services, malaria prevention and treatment, pharmaceutical supply chain strengthening, and climate-resilient health facility rehabilitation. Implemented by UNICEF through contracted NGOs.
key_components.financing: US$330.77 million (US$100.71 million IDA including US$90.49 million WHR; US$10 million Government; US$220.06 million Trust Funds)

key_components.description: Strengthening health emergency preparedness and response, laboratory systems, disease control, blood banking and transfusion services, health service quality improvement, health management information systems (DHIS2), and health sector stewardship and financing. Implemented by WHO.
key_components.financing: US$15.00 million (US$5.50 million IDA including US$3.72 million WHR; US$9.50 million Trust Funds)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Monitoring and Evaluation and Project Management
key_components.description: Third-party monitoring (TPM) of health service delivery, data visualization platform development, contract and program management capacity development for PMU, and project management operations including PMU staffing and oversight.
key_components.financing: US$23.73 million (US$10.79 million IDA WHR; US$12.94 million Trust Funds)

key_components.description: Facility for rapid financing reallocation in case of eligible emergencies (disease outbreaks, health emergencies) with potential major adverse economic/social impact. WHR resources reallocated to CERC will only benefit refugees and host communities.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Health (MoH) through Project Management Unit (PMU); UNICEF (Component 1 and pharmaceutical supply); WHO (Component 2); Third-party monitoring agencies (Component 3)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: High-Level Steering Committee (HSC) chaired by Minister of Health, co-chaired by Minister of Finance and Planning, meeting bi-annually; Operational Steering Committee (OSC) meeting quarterly with technical representatives; PMU serves as secretariat; coordination with UNHCR, Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA), humanitarian cluster, and state/county health departments
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank IPF instrument with standalone Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF) and Single-Donor Trust Fund (SDTF); UNICEF and WHO contracted as implementation partners through Output Agreements; NGOs sub-contracted by UNICEF for service delivery; competitive selection of pharmaceutical procurement and logistics agency; TPM agencies competitively selected; advance procurement authorized for major activities; Procurement Plan prepared for initial 18 months with annual updates
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Third-party monitoring (TPM) with quarterly health facility functionality assessments, data quality verification, bi-annual health service quality assessments and patient feedback surveys, biennial household coverage surveys; DHIS2 for routine data collection; data visualization platform linking TPM and DHIS2 data; quarterly progress reports from PMU, UNICEF, WHO; Geo-enabling Initiative for Monitoring and Supervision (GEMS) for near-real-time field data; Results Framework with PDO and intermediate indicators disaggregated by refugees and host communities

identified_risks.risk_description: Highly fragile context with volatile security situation, intercommunal conflicts, political uncertainty, frequent changes in political leadership and appointees that may undermine government ownership and cause project delays. Risk of political instability affecting implementation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continued engagement at multiple levels within MoH and MoFP; support for regular coordination meetings; project flexibility to allow adjustments during implementation; broad buy-in and ownership building; progressive refugee policy framework reduces refugee-specific political risks

identified_risks.risk_description: South Sudan has extremely oil-dependent, non-diversified economy; irregular payment of civil service salaries including health workers; inflation risks from commodity price shocks and currency depreciation; low foreign exchange reserves; government commitment to health financing uncertain and may not be fully adhered to; health sector chronically underfunded (only 4% of government budget vs 15% African Union target)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Enhanced stewardship of MoH and MoFP; development of clear roadmap for gradual handover of identified facilities to government; careful selection of facilities to avoid service delivery gaps; direct World Bank financing through trust funds reduces reliance on government budget execution; government committed to US$20 million co-financing (US$10 million direct allocation plus US$10 million from IMF food shock window)

identified_risks.risk_description: Continued changes in MoH leadership disrupting sectoral strategy development; international aid now integral to local political economy; however, basic health service provision is ministry priority
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong focus on procurement of necessary supplies and equipment; training of health and non-health workers in targeted areas; alignment with MoH Health Sector Strategic Plan (HSSP 2023-2027); support for health sector coordination mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of targeting refugees and host communities in conflict-affected areas; ensuring neutrality and impartiality in service delivery; managing expectations and preventing inter/intra-communal tensions over implementation; ensuring equitable access across diverse populations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Robust mechanisms for results monitoring and verification; coordination with UNHCR and CRA; conflict-sensitive approaches embedded in project design; community-based targeting mechanisms tested and adapted; strong communications campaign designed with humanitarian agencies; grievance redress mechanism; third-party monitoring to verify resources reach intended beneficiaries

identified_risks.risk_category: institutional capacity for implementation and sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: MoH and PMU managing service delivery at national level with high risk; limited government capacity for project management; weak institutional systems; need for gradual transition from external management to government-led implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Intensified capacity building program on effective project management; UNICEF and WHO subcontracted to provide service delivery and system strengthening; both agencies expanding operational and technical presence nationwide; customized institutional capacity development at MoH; PMU staffed with experienced consultants; hands-on World Bank support; expected enhanced MoH capacity by project end to lead some implementation areas; twinning approach with co-location of IP staff in county health departments

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak financial management systems at MoH; significant capacity gaps in budgetary preparation and monitoring; weak manual accounting system; deficiencies in payment authorizations and segregation of duties; lack of functional internal audit unit; previous external audit identified unresolved weaknesses including lack of bank reconciliation and non-functional computerized accounting system
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM arrangements discharged through PMU with FM specialist and project accountant; finance staff deployed by MoH to strengthen capacity; hands-on close monitoring by World Bank; UNICEF and WHO implement through their own FM systems; HACT framework for due diligence and monitoring of NGO implementing partners; Procurement Manual preparation and procurement specialist hiring as effectiveness conditions; advance procurement to fast-track implementation; World Bank HEIS support for PMU establishment

identified_risks.risk_description: High environmental and social risk classification. Environmental risks include medical waste generation and improper disposal, increased fuel consumption from mobile health services, potential deforestation from facility rehabilitation, chemical and biological hazard management. Social risks include: (a) inter/intra-communal tensions between refugees and host communities; (b) health facilities and staff becoming targets of armed groups (documented cases of facility raids and looting); (c) high prevalence of GBV and SEA/SH in conflict-affected areas; (d) potential service exclusion of vulnerable groups; (e) labor and safety risks for project workers; (f) data protection and privacy risks; (g) potential for SEA/SH by project staff or implementing partners (rated High)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) including Labor Management Procedures, GBV/SEA/SH Risk Assessment and Action Plan, General Medical Waste Management Plan, Social Assessment with Social Development Plan; Resettlement Framework; Security Risk Assessment and Management Plan; Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) and Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) disclosed November 24, 2023; environmental specialist and social specialist to be appointed within 60 days of effectiveness; grievance redress mechanism established within 60 days; conflict-sensitive approaches integrated throughout project design; community health worker program (BHI) with female BHWs to reach vulnerable populations; SGBV services and clinical management of rape training; health promotion and SBCC activities; citizen engagement and beneficiary feedback mechanisms; TPM to verify resources reach intended beneficiaries and minimize harm; coordination with humanitarian agencies; climate-adaptive and energy-efficient facility rehabilitation guidelines

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of inadequate coordination between multiple implementing partners (UNICEF, WHO, NGOs, TPM agencies); ensuring neutrality and impartiality in service delivery to refugees and host communities; managing expectations of diverse stakeholders; ensuring independence of service delivery despite government involvement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Subcontracting of UNICEF and WHO as non-state actors strengthens independence; objective methodology for procurement and distribution of supplies; communication and outreach activities to inform public and increase visibility; coordination with UNHCR and CRA; direct engagement with refugees and host communities through UNHCR platforms; HSC and OSC coordination mechanisms; liaison with Humanitarian Cluster; standardized data collection and reporting tools; lessons learned from humanitarian organizations incorporated into design

identified_risks.risk_description: Several health facilities previously attacked and looted, leading to deaths of patients and health workers. Ongoing security risks particularly in Upper Nile, Jonglei, and Unity states. Risk of facilities and staff becoming targets of armed groups.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Customized security management plan developed and implemented; coordination with security authorities; careful facility selection considering security context; mobile health services and community-based delivery (BHI) to reduce facility-based risks; staff safety protocols; incident reporting and response mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial residual risk related to data collection, processing, and privacy during project implementation. Risks include: (a) unauthorized access to personally identifiable and sensitive health information; (b) gaps in national data privacy and protection regulations; (c) cybersecurity breaches; (d) inadequate data storage and security measures
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Electronic and paper-based data collection forms with personal information stored to prevent unauthorized access; software and hardware investments to mitigate cybersecurity breach risks; compliance with applicable national regulations and good international practice; data protection protocols in Project Implementation Manual; secure data storage and backup systems; DHIS2 and data visualization platform with appropriate security measures

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 7: Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level GRM established within 60 days of effectiveness under terms satisfactory to World Bank; expands existing GRM from COVID-19 Emergency Response and Health System Preparedness Project (CERHSPP-P176480); provides inclusive, accessible, safe process for receiving and resolving grievances; closes feedback loop with communities; handles corruption and SEA allegations sensitively; provides actionable data for program adjustment. Communities can also access World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service)
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Women and girls face disproportionate burden of poverty, poor access to services, and insecurity. Project addresses: (1) High maternal mortality (1,223 per 100,000 live births - highest globally); (2) High fertility rate (4.47 births per woman) and early marriage (51% married by age 18); (3) Adolescent pregnancy (1 in 3 girls pregnant before age 15); (4) High prevalence of intimate partner violence (26.7% of ever-partnered women in past year); (5) Inadequate SGBV response with shortage of trained personnel in Clinical Management of Rape (CMR) and psycho-social support. Project interventions: (a) Strengthened delivery of sexual, reproductive, and maternal health services including family planning, ANC, skilled birth attendance, BEmONC/CEmONC, PNC; (b) Hiring and training of female Boma Health Workers (BHWs) to reach vulnerable populations; (c) SGBV services including identification, counseling, management and referral for survivors; (d) BCC on SGBV awareness and prevention; (e) Monitoring indicators: maternal mortality ratio, percentage of women receiving 4 ANC visits, percentage of deliveries attended by skilled personnel, contraceptive prevalence rate, number of GBV services provided
vulnerable_groups: Refugees (333,300 as of September 2023, 89% in Jamjang and Maban; 52% female; 81% women and children; 60% under 18 years)
Women and girls (disproportionate burden of poverty, violence, poor health outcomes)
Children under five (19% of population; under-five mortality 98.69 per 1,000 live births)
Pregnant and lactating women (high malnutrition rates: 63.4% among refugees)
Adolescent girls (early marriage, high fertility, pregnancy complications)
People with disabilities (30% of refugee population struggle with health access)
Remote and rural populations (71% live over 5km from nearest health facility)
Survivors of sexual and gender-based violence (inadequate response capacity)
Malnourished children (acute malnutrition rate 31.1% for children under 5 among refugees)
Populations affected by climate shocks (flooding, drought, high heat)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Conflict-sensitive approaches woven throughout project design. Specific measures include: (1) Equitable access mechanisms targeting refugees and host communities with conflict-sensitive planning; (2) Community-based service delivery through BHI to reach dispersed populations in conflict-affected areas; (3) Mobile health services and outreach to populations with limited access; (4) Coordination with humanitarian agencies and UNHCR to ensure neutrality and impartiality; (5) Grievance redress mechanism to address community concerns; (6) Third-party monitoring to verify equitable resource distribution; (7) Communications campaign designed with humanitarian agencies to ensure fairness perception; (8) Conflict-sensitive targeting methodology combining geographic and community-based approaches; (9) Careful facility selection considering security context; (10) Emphasis on community health workers and local engagement to build trust; (11) Integration of lessons from humanitarian organizations on ensuring neutrality in conflict-affected settings
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is one of the world's most fragile, conflict-affected and violence-prone (FCV) countries. Key fragility factors: (1) History of prolonged conflict - only 15 years of peace since 1955 (1972-1982, 2005-2011); civil war 2013-2018; ongoing intercommunal conflicts; (2) Extreme poverty - over 80% living in poverty, 60% in extreme poverty; (3) Weak institutions and governance - minimal government engagement in health (4% of budget), limited institutional capacity, weak public financial management; (4) Displacement crisis - 333,300 refugees hosted, 2.3+ million IDPs, ongoing refugee influx from Sudan conflict (April 2023); (5) Climate vulnerability - highest globally; 7th most vulnerable to riverine flooding; record flooding 2019-2022 displacing 300,000; (6) Limited infrastructure - 80% rural population, limited road access, disrupted by annual flooding; (7) Insecurity and violence - documented attacks on health facilities and workers; armed group activity in Upper Nile, Jonglei, Unity states; (8) Weak health system - fragmented service delivery, minimal government stewardship, heavy reliance on external financing and humanitarian actors; (9) Poor health outcomes - highest maternal mortality globally (1,223 per 100,000), highest neonatal mortality (39.63 per 1,000), under-five mortality 98.69 per 1,000; (10) Food insecurity - 5.8 million (over 50% of population) in acute food insecurity crisis or worse as of November 2023
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security risks are significant and ongoing. Documented incidents include: (1) Health facilities attacked and looted with deaths of patients and health workers; (2) Particular risks in Upper Nile, Jonglei, and Unity states; (3) Armed group activity targeting health infrastructure and personnel; (4) Volatile intercommunal conflicts; (5) Refugee influx from Sudan conflict creating additional security pressures. Mitigation measures: (1) Customized security management plan developed and implemented; (2) Careful facility selection considering security context; (3) Mobile health services and community-based delivery (BHI) to reduce facility-based exposure; (4) Staff safety protocols and incident reporting mechanisms; (5) Coordination with security authorities; (6) Flexibility in implementation to adapt to changing security situation; (7) UNICEF positioned to maintain service delivery if conflict resumes/intensifies; (8) Hands-on World Bank support and monitoring
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Significant displacement and refugee populations. Details: (1) Refugee population: 333,300 as of September 2023 (UNHCR data), with 311,160 Sudanese refugees (93% of total) arriving since April 2023 Sudan conflict; (2) Geographic concentration: 89% in two locations - Jamjang (Pariang County, Ruweng Administrative Area) and Bunj (Maban County, Upper Nile State); (3) Demographics: 52% female, 81% women and children, 60% under 18 years, 38% aged 18-59, remainder over 60; (4) Internally displaced persons: 2.3+ million IDPs from internal conflicts and climate shocks; (5) Projected growth: UNHCR estimates 446,625 refugees by 2024 and 456,496 by 2025; (6) Health vulnerabilities: Acute malnutrition 31.1% for children under 5 and 63.4% for pregnant/lactating women; measles outbreak in Renk (59 deaths as of September 2023); malaria 40% of medical consultations; (7) Host community stress: Refugee-hosting areas already under stress from internal displacement and flooding; humanitarian agencies scaling down support; (8) Government policy: South Sudan has progressive refugee framework - acceded to 1951 Refugee Convention and 1967 Protocol, 1969 OAU Convention; Refugee Act 2012; grants refugees freedom of movement, land access, livelihood opportunities; Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) coordinates government support; (9) Project response: US$105 million from IDA Window for Host Communities and Refugees (WHR); expansion of services to refugee and host community areas beyond previous Maban and Jamjang coverage; targeted health service delivery including maternal/child health, nutrition, immunization, SGBV services; community health worker expansion; coordination with UNHCR and CRA; transition plan for 50 facilities being handed over by humanitarian agencies; monitoring disaggregated by refugee and host community populations
